title: Sgraffito abstract tile
subtitle: A white-slipped tile with bold abstract lines scratched through to the body clay
excerpt: A flat clay tile painted with white slip while still soft, allowed to firm to leather-hard, then decorated with bold abstract lines scratched through the slip to reveal the darker body beneath. Air-dry clay, no kiln required.
sourceNotes: Sgraffito technique derived from first principles. Historical sgraffito slipware reference: V&A open-access collection.

content.text: A
sgraffito
tile uses the contrast between a white
slip

layer and the darker body clay beneath it. This tutorial focuses on bold abstract lines (curves, spirals, hatching) rather than figurative drawing, which makes the scratching process more forgiving for a first attempt. Air-dry clay in its natural grey-brown colour reads well against white slip.

items.name: White air-dry clay for slip
items.substitution: White acrylic paint thinned to slip consistency

content.text: Making the tile
1. Roll and cut

Roll the grey clay to 8 mm thickness and cut a square or rectangle: 12 cm by 12 cm works well for bold abstract decoration. A thicker tile at 8 mm rather than 6 mm is more resistant to warping as it dries flat.
2. Apply the white slip while still soft
Thin the white clay in water to single cream consistency. Paint two coats of white
slip
onto the tile surface using a wide flat brush. Apply the second coat when the first is surface-dry. The slip layer must cover the tile surface evenly in a layer about 1 mm thick.
3. Wait for leather-hard
Leave the slipped tile to firm to
leather-hard
before scratching. The white slip should look matte and pale rather than glossy and wet. At this stage, a needle tool will scratch the slip cleanly without smearing.
4. Scratch the design

Draw the abstract pattern into the white slip with the needle tool, exposing the dark grey body clay beneath. Abstract starting points: a loose spiral from the centre outward; broad curved lines following the tile edge; a bold grid of hatched lines at 45 degrees. Work confidently; hesitant lines produce jagged edges, firm single strokes cut cleanly.
Drying and finishing
Dry the tile flat on a plaster bat or weighted board to prevent warping. Once bone-dry, seal with PVA on all surfaces including the back. Two coats of matte PVA preserves the tile's natural look.
Variations
Reverse the colours: apply dark slip (acrylic black in the base slip) over white clay, then scratch through to reveal the white body.
After scratching, brush a thin acrylic colour wash into the scratched lines to add a third tone.

items.fix: At leather-hard the slip should be firmly bonded; apply slip to the tile immediately after rolling, while still soft
items.cause: Slip was applied too thickly or the tile was too wet when slip was applied

items.fix: Dry on a flat plaster bat; cover with a light plastic sheet for the first day
items.cause: Tile dried too quickly on one side
items.symptom: Tile cracks across the surface as it dries
